note: Per-peptide adaptive intake contract. RESEARCH-POPULATED: every clinical claim must carry a real citation (FDA label / PubMed DOI-PMID / guideline) or be marked VERIFY — never fabricated. This data drives the workspace intake, the advisories, the consent tier, AND the sellable PDF intake template in each peptide's Practice Pack.

override_policy.principle: The provider can override ANY recommendation. AllyOS is decision-support, never a gatekeeper. Every override is a documented clinical decision by the licensed provider — which is what keeps the platform a tool and transfers responsibility to the provider.
override_policy.hard_stop: blocking modal requiring a TYPED override rationale; recorded in the note + consent. Highest friction, still overridable — not an absolute lock.

all_patients: weight-related comorbidities (HTN, dyslipidemia, prediabetes/T2DM, OSA, CVD, MASLD)
goal / indication (and basis: clinical vs preference)

indication_questions: focused goal-fit / why-here questions
agent_specific_screen: red-flag questions specific to this agent (pill-friendly)
hard_stops: agent-specific blocking conditions (overridable with typed rationale)

obesity_blunts_gh_axis.rule: obesity suppresses GH/GHRH response; reversible with weight loss

gh_igf1_malignancy.rule: GH/IGF-1 mitogenic; active malignancy = platform hard stop
gh_igf1_malignancy.source: acromegaly neoplasia epidemiology (PubMed) — VERIFY specific refs

sermorelin.name: Sermorelin
indication_questions: What is your primary goal? (e.g., body composition / fat loss, recovery, energy/vitality, sleep, 'anti-aging')
Is this driven by a specific clinical concern documented by a clinician, or by personal preference / word-of-mouth / online research?
Have you ever had IGF-1 (and/or GH) tested? If so, what was the result and when? Bring the report if available.
Has any clinician ever diagnosed you with adult growth-hormone deficiency or pituitary disease, or worked you up for it?

Which symptoms are you experiencing, and for how long? (low energy, reduced muscle, increased central fat, poor recovery, sleep disturbance) Are there other explanations (poor sleep, overtraining, low testosterone/thyroid, depression, weight gain)?
What objective change would tell you this is working, and over what timeframe?
agent_specific_screen: Active or prior malignancy, or any undiagnosed mass / cancer red-flag symptoms (unintended weight loss, night sweats, new lump)?
Height and weight to compute BMI (obesity blunts the GH axis and the GH response to secretagogues, lowering the chance of response)?
Known impaired fasting glucose, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, or insulin resistance? (GH/IGF-1 can worsen insulin sensitivity and raise glucose)
Untreated or undertreated hypothyroidism? (must be corrected first or you mislabel a non-responder and confound IGF-1)
Diagnosed or suspected obstructive sleep apnea, or significant snoring/daytime somnolence? (GH-axis stimulation can aggravate fluid retention/OSA)
Personal or family history of pituitary tumor / non-functional pituitary (would not respond to a GHRH analog)?
hard_stops: Pregnancy or positive pregnancy test (platform hard stop; overridable only with typed clinical rationale)
Active malignancy (platform hard stop given GH/IGF-1 mitogenicity; overridable only with typed clinical rationale)
Untreated severe hypothyroidism (treat and re-confirm before initiating; overridable with typed rationale)

soft_advisories.message: Obesity itself suppresses spontaneous GH secretion and blunts the GH response to GHRH/secretagogues, so a poor response is expected and this is largely a reversible, acquired defect that normalizes with substantial weight loss. Address weight and metabolic health first (lifestyle, and/or a GLP-1/incretin agent), which restores the GH/IGF-1 axis. If the actual goal is visceral fat reduction, tesamorelin is the evidence-backed GHRH analog (FDA-approved for HIV-associated visceral adiposity) rather than sermorelin.
soft_advisories.citation: Rasmussen 1995, J Clin Endocrinol Metab, doi:10.1210/jcem.80.4.7536210; Nam 1996, Metabolism, doi:10.1016/s0026-0495(96)90029-3; Douyon & Schteingart 2002, Endocrinol Metab Clin North Am, doi:10.1016/s0889-8529(01)00023-8; tesamorelin: Stanley 2012, Clin Infect Dis, doi:10.1093/cid/cis251

soft_advisories.trigger: Normal/age-appropriate IGF-1 OR no diagnosed GH deficiency (preference-driven request)
soft_advisories.message: This is elective wellness use without a diagnosed deficiency. Sermorelin is not FDA-approved for adult anti-aging/optimization and the human evidence for benefit in this setting is weak; the best RCT-level data are for recombinant GH in healthy older adults, where only small body-composition changes were seen alongside increased adverse events, and the authors concluded GH cannot be recommended as anti-aging therapy. Set expectations accordingly: modest at best, not proven, off-label. Define an objective endpoint and a stop rule before starting.

soft_advisories.trigger: Impaired fasting glucose / prediabetes / T2DM / insulin resistance
soft_advisories.message: Raising GH/IGF-1 can reduce insulin sensitivity and worsen glycemia; in RCTs of GH in healthy older adults, treated participants were more likely to develop impaired fasting glucose and diabetes. Optimize glucose first, monitor fasting glucose/A1c on therapy, and reconsider if glycemia worsens.

soft_advisories.trigger: Any consideration of sustained supraphysiologic IGF-1
soft_advisories.message: Higher circulating IGF-1 is associated in large prospective cohorts with increased risk of several cancers (colorectal, breast, prostate, thyroid). Do not push IGF-1 above the age-appropriate upper-normal range; titrate to symptoms and upper-normal IGF-1, not beyond.
soft_advisories.citation: Knuppel 2020, Cancer Res (UK Biobank, 30 cancers), doi:10.1158/0008-5472.CAN-20-1281

baseline_labs: Fasting glucose and HbA1c (metabolic safety baseline)
TSH +/- free T4 (correct hypothyroidism before interpreting IGF-1 or response)

monitoring.check: Tolerance, injection-site reactions, fluid retention/arthralgia/paresthesia (signs of excess), sleep
Symptom response and fasting glucose; hold or reinforce timing
IGF-1 (titrate toward upper-normal-for-age, not above) and fasting glucose/A1c
IGF-1, HbA1c, body composition; use lowest effective dose and stop if no objective change

consent_tier.rationale: Off-label, compounded GH secretagogue used for adult wellness/'GH-axis optimization' without an FDA-approved indication for this use (brand Geref was approved only for pediatric GHD diagnosis/treatment and is discontinued). Adult-optimization evidence is weak (Grade C/D); most use is preference-driven without a diagnosed deficiency, and there are real metabolic (glucose) and theoretical mitogenicity considerations. Requires enhanced off-label informed consent (consent_gh_secretagogue), honest expectation-setting, and documented exclusion of malignancy/pregnancy/untreated hypothyroidism.

evidence.grade: B (pediatric GHD diagnosis/treatment, historical FDA-approved Geref) / D (adult wellness 'optimization')
evidence.summary: Sermorelin is GHRH(1-29); as brand Geref it was FDA-approved for pediatric GH-deficiency diagnosis and treatment and is now discontinued, so all current adult use is compounded and off-label. There are no RCTs supporting sermorelin for adult anti-aging/body-composition 'optimization'; the closest high-quality evidence is a systematic review/meta-analysis of recombinant GH in healthy older adults, which found only small body-composition changes with more adverse events (edema, arthralgia, carpal tunnel, impaired fasting glucose/diabetes) and concluded GH cannot be recommended as anti-aging therapy. Because obesity suppresses the GH axis and blunts the secretagogue response (reversibly with weight loss), candidates with BMI>=30 are unlikely to respond and should address weight/metabolic health first; tesamorelin is the only GHRH analog with RCT-grade evidence for (visceral) fat, in HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Net: in adults without a diagnosed, worked-up GH deficiency, the evidence for benefit is weak and the use is elective.

citations.label: Liu 2007, Ann Intern Med — Systematic review: safety/efficacy of GH in the healthy elderly (GH not recommended as anti-aging; more adverse events incl. impaired glucose/diabetes)
Rasmussen 1995, J Clin Endocrinol Metab — Massive weight loss restores 24-h GH release and IGF-I in obese subjects (obesity GH defect is reversible)
Nam 1996, Metabolism — FFA suppression potentiates blunted GH response to GHRH in obesity
Douyon & Schteingart 2002, Endocrinol Metab Clin North Am — Obesity lowers GH and blunts GH response to GHRH; reversed by weight reduction
Knuppel 2020, Cancer Res — Circulating IGF-I and risk of 30 cancers (UK Biobank)
Stanley 2012, Clin Infect Dis — Tesamorelin (GHRH analog) reduces visceral fat with metabolic improvement, HIV lipodystrophy RCT data
Dhillon 2011, Drugs — Tesamorelin review (only approved GHRH analog for visceral adiposity)
Prakash & Goa 1999, BioDrugs — Sermorelin (Geref) review: approved use was pediatric GHD diagnosis/treatment

tirzepatide.name: Tirzepatide
indication_questions: What is the clinical goal here — type 2 diabetes glycemic control, chronic weight management, both, or weight-loss preference without a qualifying indication?
What is the calculated BMI? (Label indication for weight management: BMI ≥30, OR ≥27 with at least one weight-related comorbidity such as HTN, dyslipidemia, prediabetes/T2DM, OSA, or CVD.)
If BMI 27–29.9: which weight-related comorbidity establishes indication fit?
Most recent A1c / known type 2 diabetes? (Mounjaro indication; informs hypoglycemia risk if on insulin/secretagogue.)
Any prior incretin/GLP-1 experience (semaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutide, or prior tirzepatide)? Maximum dose tolerated and reason for stopping?
Prior GI intolerance to incretins (nausea, vomiting, severe constipation/diarrhea) — severity and whether it resolved with slower titration?
Diagnosed moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea with obesity? (Zepbound is FDA-approved for OSA in this population — supports indication and goal-setting.)
Known heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) with obesity? (Emerging benefit; document as a treatment-relevant context, not a separate label indication for compounded/weight use.)
agent_specific_screen: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC)? (Boxed warning — contraindicated.)
Personal or family history of Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2)? (Boxed warning — contraindicated.)
History of pancreatitis (acute or chronic)? Any recent/persistent severe abdominal pain radiating to the back?
Gallbladder disease — known gallstones, prior cholecystitis, or cholecystectomy? (Increased acute gallbladder disease on therapy.)
Severe gastrointestinal disease — gastroparesis or other severe GI motility disorder? (Delayed gastric emptying; not formally studied in severe GI disease.)
Type 1 diabetes or history of diabetic ketoacidosis? (Not a substitute for insulin; not indicated for T1DM.)

Currently using ORAL contraceptives? (Tirzepatide delays gastric emptying and can reduce oral contraceptive absorption — switch to a non-oral method or add a barrier method for 4 weeks after initiation and for 4 weeks after each dose escalation.)
On insulin or an insulin secretagogue (sulfonylurea, glinide)? (Increased hypoglycemia risk — plan dose reduction at initiation.)
History of diabetic retinopathy requiring treatment? (Not studied in proliferative retinopathy / macular edema; monitor with rapid glycemic improvement.)
hard_stops: Pregnancy or positive pregnancy test (platform hard stop; discontinue and counsel — weight loss offers no benefit and there is potential fetal harm). Overridable only with typed clinical rationale.
Active malignancy (platform hard stop). Overridable only with typed clinical rationale.
Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) — FDA boxed-warning contraindication. Overridable only with typed clinical rationale.
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2) — FDA boxed-warning contraindication. Overridable only with typed clinical rationale.
Known serious hypersensitivity to tirzepatide or any excipient. Overridable only with typed clinical rationale.

soft_advisories.trigger: Concomitant insulin or insulin secretagogue (sulfonylurea / glinide)
soft_advisories.message: Increased hypoglycemia risk in combination with an insulin secretagogue (10.3% vs 2.1% without). Consider reducing the dose of insulin or secretagogue at initiation and counsel on hypoglycemia recognition.

Tirzepatide delays gastric emptying and may reduce oral contraceptive absorption. Advise switching to a non-oral contraceptive or adding a barrier method for 4 weeks after initiation and for 4 weeks after each dose escalation.
soft_advisories.citation: Zepbound USPI, Drug Interactions / Use in Specific Populations (DailyMed): https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=487cd7e7-434c-4925-99fa-aa80b1cc776b

soft_advisories.message: Patient is below the labeled weight-management indication (BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with a weight-related comorbidity). Reassess indication fit, goal appropriateness, and eating-disorder screen before proceeding; document basis for any off-label use.

Acute pancreatitis has been reported with incretin therapy. Use caution with a pancreatitis history; counsel patient to report persistent severe abdominal pain (± radiating to back), and discontinue if pancreatitis is suspected.

Acute gallbladder disease (cholelithiasis, cholecystitis) occurs more frequently on therapy and with rapid weight loss. Counsel on right-upper-quadrant pain, fever, and jaundice; evaluate if symptomatic.

soft_advisories.trigger: Significant GI adverse events (vomiting / diarrhea) or volume depletion
soft_advisories.message: GI adverse reactions can cause dehydration and, rarely, acute kidney injury (postmarketing). Counsel on hydration; hold and reassess renal function during severe or persistent GI symptoms.
soft_advisories.citation: Zepbound USPI, Warnings & Precautions — Acute Kidney Injury (DailyMed): https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=487cd7e7-434c-4925-99fa-aa80b1cc776b

soft_advisories.message: Prefer FDA-approved Mounjaro/Zepbound pen or vial (fixed-concentration, no reconstitution math). If a compounded vial is used, verify concentration and source-pharmacy legitimacy before dosing.
soft_advisories.citation: FDA — compounded GLP-1 safety communications; Zepbound USPI dosage form (DailyMed): https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=487cd7e7-434c-4925-99fa-aa80b1cc776b

baseline_labs: Comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) — includes renal function and hepatic enzymes
Lipase ± amylase (optional; baseline if pancreatitis history or to anchor future workup)
Pregnancy test (urine or serum) in persons of childbearing potential before initiation
Screen (history) for personal/family MTC or MEN 2 and pancreatitis — gating

monitoring.when: Baseline / initiation (2.5 mg SC weekly, non-therapeutic starting dose)
monitoring.check: Confirm indication fit and contraindication screen; review baseline labs and pregnancy status; reduce insulin/secretagogue dose; counsel on GI side effects, contraception, and pancreatitis red flags.
GI tolerance, hydration, injection technique/site, hypoglycemia if on insulin/SU; advance to 5 mg only if tolerated; reinforce barrier contraception window after each escalation.
Tolerance and weight trend; titrate by tolerance to the lowest effective maintenance dose (max 15 mg weekly), not by the calendar; re-counsel contraception backup for 4 weeks after each step.
A1c (if diabetic) and percent weight change vs goal; blood pressure; GI/gallbladder symptoms; adjust toward target dose.

A1c, weight maintenance, lipids, renal function (CMP); reassess effective maintenance dose, muscle-preservation/protein + resistance plan, and continued indication; counsel that weight regain is expected if discontinued.

consent_tier.rationale: FDA-approved (Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes; Zepbound for chronic weight management and for moderate-to-severe OSA with obesity), used on-label within its indication. Grade A randomized-trial evidence and an established label; standard informed consent. Escalates to a higher tier only if used off-label (e.g., BMI below threshold) or sourced from a compounding pharmacy.

evidence.summary: Large, completed Phase 3 program. SURMOUNT-1: up to -20.9% body weight at 72 weeks with 15 mg in obesity. SURPASS-2: superior A1c reduction (up to -2.30%) and greater weight loss vs semaglutide 1 mg in type 2 diabetes. SURMOUNT-OSA: ~-20 to -24 events/hour AHI reduction in moderate-to-severe OSA with obesity (supporting FDA OSA approval). SUMMIT: reduced composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure (HR 0.62) and improved KCCQ in HFpEF with obesity. Most common adverse events are GI and dose-escalation-related; boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors (rodent), contraindicated in MTC/MEN 2.

citations.label: Zepbound (tirzepatide) US Prescribing Information — DailyMed (FDA label: contraindications, boxed warning, pancreatitis, gallbladder, hypoglycemia, oral-contraceptive, titration)
Mounjaro (tirzepatide) US Prescribing Information — DailyMed (T2DM label)
Jastreboff 2022, NEJM — SURMOUNT-1 (obesity, PMID 35658024)
Frías 2021, NEJM — SURPASS-2 (tirzepatide vs semaglutide in T2DM, PMID 34170647)
Malhotra 2024, NEJM — SURMOUNT-OSA (OSA + obesity, PMID 38912654)
Packer 2024, NEJM — SUMMIT (HFpEF + obesity, PMID 39555826)

semaglutide.name: Semaglutide
indication_questions: What is your treatment goal here: chronic weight management, type 2 diabetes/glucose control, or both?
What is your current height and weight? (used to compute BMI for indication fit)
Is your BMI >=30, OR >=27 with a weight-related condition (HTN, dyslipidemia, prediabetes/T2DM, OSA, MASLD, established CVD)? (yes/no)
Which weight-related conditions do you have, if any? (HTN, high cholesterol, prediabetes/T2DM, sleep apnea, fatty liver, heart disease)
Have you used a GLP-1 (semaglutide, tirzepatide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) before? (yes/no)
If yes: highest dose reached, how long, and why you stopped (effect, cost, side effects)?
How did you tolerate it, especially nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea? (well / moderate / poorly)
Are you currently on insulin or a sulfonylurea (e.g., glipizide, glimepiride)? (yes/no)
Any current nausea, vomiting, early fullness, or known slow stomach emptying? (yes/no)
agent_specific_screen: Personal history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC)? (yes/no)
Family history of MTC or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 (MEN2)? (yes/no)
Prior episode of pancreatitis? (yes/no)
Known gallstones / gallbladder disease, or prior gallbladder symptoms? (yes/no)
Severe gastroparesis or other significant motility disorder? (yes/no)
Type 1 diabetes, or any history of diabetic ketoacidosis? (yes/no)
Established diabetic retinopathy (especially proliferative or being treated)? (yes/no)
Prior serious allergic/hypersensitivity reaction to semaglutide or another GLP-1? (yes/no)
Active or recent eating disorder, or rapid restrictive dieting? (yes/no)
Are you pregnant, planning pregnancy within ~2 months, or breastfeeding? (yes/no)
Significant kidney disease or recent dehydration/volume depletion? (yes/no)
hard_stops: Pregnancy or positive pregnancy test (PLATFORM hard stop — overridable only with typed clinical rationale)
Active malignancy (PLATFORM hard stop — overridable only with typed clinical rationale)
Personal history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) (boxed warning / labeled contraindication — overridable only with typed rationale)
Family history of MTC or MEN2 (boxed warning / labeled contraindication — overridable only with typed rationale)
Prior serious hypersensitivity reaction to semaglutide (labeled contraindication — overridable only with typed rationale)

soft_advisories.message: Increased hypoglycemia risk. Consider reducing the insulin or sulfonylurea dose at initiation and during titration, and counsel on hypoglycemia recognition/treatment.

soft_advisories.trigger: BMI < 25 (or BMI < 27 with no weight-related comorbidity) entered for a weight indication
soft_advisories.message: Below the labeled chronic-weight-management indication threshold (BMI >=30, or >=27 with a weight-related comorbidity). Reassess indication fit and goal; document rationale if proceeding off-label.

soft_advisories.trigger: Person of reproductive potential not on reliable contraception
soft_advisories.message: Semaglutide is contraindicated in pregnancy (animal embryofetal harm); discontinue at least ~2 months before a planned pregnancy given the ~1-week half-life. Counsel on effective contraception. Delayed gastric emptying can reduce absorption of co-administered oral medications; for ORAL semaglutide (Rybelsus) and at initiation/dose changes, advise a non-oral or back-up contraceptive method.

soft_advisories.trigger: Prior pancreatitis or new severe/persistent abdominal pain
soft_advisories.message: Acute pancreatitis has been reported with GLP-1 RAs. Use caution with a pancreatitis history; if pancreatitis is suspected, stop semaglutide and evaluate. Do not restart if confirmed.

soft_advisories.trigger: History of cholelithiasis / gallbladder disease, or RUQ symptoms during rapid weight loss
soft_advisories.message: Acute gallbladder disease (cholelithiasis/cholecystitis) occurs more often with semaglutide and with rapid weight loss. Counsel on RUQ pain, fever, jaundice; evaluate if symptomatic.

Rapid glucose improvement can transiently worsen diabetic retinopathy; SUSTAIN-6 showed more retinopathy complications with semaglutide in high-risk T2D (HR 1.76, 95% CI 1.11-2.78). Ensure recent eye exam and monitor patients with a history of retinopathy.

soft_advisories.trigger: Significant ongoing nausea/vomiting/diarrhea or volume depletion
soft_advisories.message: GI losses can cause dehydration and acute kidney injury (sometimes requiring dialysis), usually in those with pre-existing renal impairment. Hydrate, slow titration, and monitor renal function in at-risk patients.

baseline_labs: Comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) — renal function, electrolytes, hepatic panel
Lipase (if pancreatitis history or significant abdominal symptoms)
Pregnancy test (serum or urine) in persons of reproductive potential

monitoring.check: BMI/weight, A1c, fasting glucose, CMP (renal/hepatic), lipids, pregnancy test if applicable; review MTC/MEN2, pancreatitis, gallbladder, retinopathy, insulin/SU use
GI tolerance (nausea/vomiting/diarrhea), hydration, weight trend; hold or slow dose escalation if not tolerated; reduce insulin/SU if hypoglycemia
A1c (if diabetic), weight-loss response (assess >=5% threshold for continued benefit), BP, renal function if at risk; reassess if inadequate response
A1c, weight maintenance, lipids, renal function; ongoing surveillance for pancreatitis, gallbladder symptoms, and retinopathy (in T2D); confirm contraception/pregnancy status as relevant

consent_tier.rationale: FDA-approved, on-label use (chronic weight management at BMI >=30 or >=27 with a weight-related comorbidity; type 2 diabetes; and associated CV/renal/MASH indications) is standard-of-care, Grade A, and warrants standard informed consent (Tier 1). Off-label use (e.g., BMI below threshold without comorbidity, or compounded semaglutide) should be escalated to Tier 2 with documented rationale; the MTC/MEN2 boxed-warning override always requires a typed clinical rationale.

evidence.summary: According to PubMed, semaglutide has an extensive Phase 3 evidence base. In overweight/obesity, once-weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg produced ~15% mean weight loss vs placebo over 68 weeks (STEP 1) with durable effect at 2 years (STEP 5). In high-CV-risk patients with overweight/obesity WITHOUT diabetes, semaglutide reduced major adverse cardiovascular events (SELECT). In type 2 diabetes, SUSTAIN-6 showed MACE reduction but a significant increase in diabetic-retinopathy complications (HR 1.76, 95% CI 1.11-2.78). FLOW demonstrated a 24% reduction in major kidney-disease events in T2D with CKD. A Phase 3 trial in MASH with fibrosis stage 2-3 (ESSENCE) showed steatohepatitis resolution in 62.9% vs 34.3% and fibrosis improvement in 36.8% vs 22.4%. FDA-label safety anchors include a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell (medullary) tumors, contraindications (personal/family MTC or MEN2; serious hypersensitivity), and warnings for pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, hypoglycemia with insulin/secretagogues, diabetic-retinopathy worsening, acute kidney injury, and pregnancy contraindication.

citations.label: FDA Wegovy (semaglutide) Prescribing Information — Drugs@FDA (BLA/NDA 215256): boxed warning, contraindications, warnings (pancreatitis, gallbladder, hypoglycemia, retinopathy, AKI), indication/BMI threshold, titration, pregnancy
FDA Ozempic (semaglutide) Prescribing Information — Drugs@FDA (NDA 209637): T2D indication, dosing/titration, CV risk reduction
FDA Rybelsus (oral semaglutide) Prescribing Information — Drugs@FDA (NDA 213051): oral dosing, fasting administration, gastric-emptying/oral-drug absorption
Wilding 2021, NEJM — STEP 1: once-weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg in overweight/obesity (PubMed; PMID 33567185)
Garvey 2022, Nat Med — STEP 5: 2-year semaglutide in overweight/obesity (PubMed; PMID 36216945)
Lincoff 2023, NEJM — SELECT: CV outcomes in obesity without diabetes (PubMed; PMID 37952131)
Marso 2016, NEJM — SUSTAIN-6: CV outcomes in T2D; retinopathy HR 1.76 (PubMed; PMID 27633186)
Perkovic 2024, NEJM — FLOW: renal outcomes in T2D with CKD (PubMed; PMID 38785209)
Sanyal 2025, NEJM — ESSENCE Phase 3: semaglutide in MASH with fibrosis (PubMed; PMID 40305708)
